sentences.text: Yuna is four months old and leaves Korea.
She has no memories of the country where she was born.
She has no memories of the language her parents spoke.
She has no memories of the food, sounds, and smells.
Yuna had a Korean name and a small photo of her mother.
Yuna grew up in a small town in America.
It was a place where most people were white.
Her parents are white and they adopt her.
Her sister from Korea is the only Asian person she knows.
They lived on a quiet street with nice neighbors.
They went to a school with children who are white.
They went to a church with families that are white.
When Yuna was five, her teacher did an exercise.
She asked the children to stand if they had the same hair.
Most children stand up and look around.
She asked them to stand if their eyes matched their mother's.
Most children stand up again and smile.
Yuna sits in her chair and looks around.
She looked very different from her adoptive parents.
She has black hair and dark eyes.
They have blonde hair and blue eyes.
She is the only child who cannot match.
The teacher asked many questions during the class.
Yuna felt hot on her face.
She felt that everyone looked at her.
She finally tells a lie.
She said her father had black hair and went to lunch.
But she knows the truth now.
She is different from others and feels alone.
She feels alone and thinks she does not belong.
In second grade, Yuna's school had a special Heritage Day.
Each child brings something from their culture to share.
Yuna is excited and feels happy.
Her mother helps her make kimchi, a Korean food.
They prepared it with care and attention.
They put it in a nice box.
Yuna is excited and wants to share it with her classmates.
When Yuna brought kimchi to school, the other children made faces.
They said it smells bad.
They said it looked bad and not nice.
They said they do not want to try it.
Yuna feels excited but then feels shame.
They did not like her food but ate it anyway.
She felt that they did not like her.
She threw the kimchi away and never brought it to school again.
Yuna tried harder to fit in as she got older.
She stopped using the few Korean words that she knew.
Yuna did not go to Korean camp in the summer because her parents wanted her to.
She spent all her time with her friends in Korea.
She tried to be like them and act the same way.
Talk like them and you will understand better.
Dress like them and you will feel good.
She tried hard, but she could not change her face.
She cannot change her eyes and feels sad.
She cannot change that she is Korean.
When Yuna was fifteen, something important happened in her life.
Yuna watches a Korean drama on her computer at night.
The characters speak Korean and use it to talk.
Yuna cannot understand any words when she listens.
But she felt something very strange.
I feel a pull to go outside.
Yuna feels a strong longing for her home in Korea.
Yuna stayed awake all night because she did not understand Korean.
The next morning, Yuna wakes up and makes a decision.
She teaches herself Korean and learns new words every day.
She found a website that teaches the Korean alphabet.
She printed out the worksheets for her class.
She practiced every day after school and got better.
Her parents were surprised but they supported her.
They buy her books to learn Korean language.
They found a tutor from Korea on the internet.
Learning Korean is hard but fun.
The grammar is very different from English.
The sounds are hard to make.
Yuna feels bad because she makes no progress.
But she continued to move forward.
She watches Korean dramas and enjoys them very much.
She listens to Korean music and enjoys it.
She practiced writing Korean letters and felt happy.
She slowly starts to understand what is happening.
When Yuna was twenty-two, she chose to go to Korea.
She found a program that helps Korean adoptees return to Korea.
She applied for a job and hopes to get it.
She is happy because she was accepted.
She buys a plane ticket to go on a trip.
She packs her bags for the trip.
She says goodbye to her family.
The flight is long and takes a lot of time.
Yuna cannot sleep at night and feels very tired.
She feels nervous when she speaks in front of people.
What is Korea like and how does it feel?
Does she feel at home in Korea?
Will people accept Yuna when she comes?
Does she finally belong in Korea or Daejeon?
Yuna arrives in Seoul on her birthday.
She thought it was important and had a special meaning.
This is a new beginning for Yuna and her friends.
A new life begins.
She got off the plane and walked in the airport.
For the first time, she was with people who looked like her.
She has dark hair and it looks nice.
Yuna has dark eyes that shine like stars.
Korean faces are everywhere in Seoul and Daejeon.
She took a taxi to her hotel in Seoul.
She checks in at the hotel.
She went to her room and closed the door.
She feels very tired after a long day.
She fell asleep right away and did not wake up.
Yuna wakes up early in the morning.
She turns on the television to watch her favorite show.
Every channel shows Korean people on TV.
Korean news anchors read the news and share important stories.
Korean actors work in movies and shows in Korea.
Korean singers make music and perform in many places.
Korean faces are everywhere in Korea and in Seoul.
Yuna sat on the bed and cried softly.
She never saw herself like this before.
In America, she was often the only Asian person there.
She was everywhere in Korea and Yuna felt it.
She is normal and lives in Korea.
She felt she belonged to her family and friends.
Yuna explored Seoul for two weeks and had fun.
She walked through the busy streets and saw many people.
She ate Korean food at small restaurants in Seoul.
She visited temples and palaces in Korea.
Yuna tried to speak Korean with shopkeepers and waiters.
Sometimes they understand her and feel her emotions.
They sometimes laugh at her accent and it makes her sad.
They were always nice and kind to everyone.
One day, Yuna took a train to her city.
It was named Daejeon.
She has never been there before.
She only saw the name on her papers before.
When she steps off the train, she looks around.
The buildings in Seoul are very tall and impressive.
The mountains are far away in the distance.
People walk everywhere in the city and enjoy the day.
Yuna felt a strange feeling and did not know why.
A feeling of coming home is warm and nice.
But there is also loneliness.
She has no family here in Korea.
Yuna has no friends and feels lonely.
No one is there to welcome her back.
She wanted to shout to the people walking by that she is back.
I am back after twenty-two years, but I feel happy.
No one missed me, but they just walked away.
They did not know her name or where she lived.
They did not know she was gone.
Yuna walks in Daejeon for two hours.
She did not know where to go or what to do.
She did not know what to find or look for.
She feels thankful but is also sad.
Daejeon is now more than just a name on paper.
It was a real place and people visited it.
She was there before.
She walked the streets of the city.
She breathes the air and feels alive.
Yuna leaves Korea and decides to climb a mountain.
Hallasan is the tallest mountain in Korea.
Yuna never climbed a mountain before this time.
But she felt she must do it.
A physical test matches her feelings on the journey.
The climb was harder for Yuna than she thought.
The path is steep and hard to walk.
Her legs hurt and she feels tired.
Her lungs burn when she runs fast.
She wanted to give up but kept trying.
But she continued to move forward.
You move forward step by step to reach your goal.
We breathe in and out, one breath at a time.
She thinks about her journey and feels happy.
Yuna learns Korean and enjoys speaking with friends.
I come to Korea and see many beautiful places.
Yuna finds herself when she looks inside her heart.
Each step is a part of her story.
Yuna reached the top after six long hours.
She stood and gasped for air while looking at the world.
She feels the wind on her face.
She saw clouds moving around her in the sky.
She did it and felt happy.
She climbs the mountain and feels happy.
She comes back to Korea.
She reconnects with her heritage and feels happy.
When it was time to leave Korea, Yuna felt sad.
She does not want to go.
On the plane, she looked out and saw Seoul below.
She began to cry because she felt very sad.
Yuna cried for twenty-two years because she missed her heritage.
She cried because she lost her language and felt sad.
She cried for the culture she left behind and missed.
Yuna cried when she left a place that felt like home.
But she also feels something different.
Hope is a feeling that helps us in hard times.
She reconnects with her roots and feels happy.
She learned her language and felt happy.
She visited her birth country, Korea, a long time ago.
She saw her reflection everywhere and felt strange.
She finds a part of herself that was lost.
Yuna knew she would return to Korea one day.
She knows she will keep learning Korean.
She knew she would explore her heritage more and more.
Now she understands something important and feels happy.
She is not only American but also from Korea.
She is not only Korean but also from Daejeon.
She was both happy and sad at the same time.
That was okay and everything was fine.
That was very beautiful and made me happy.
Yuna started a group for Korean adoptees in America.
She wanted to help people connect with their culture.
She wanted to share what she learned with others.
She wanted to make a place where people feel understood.
Yuna started to speak Korean more often and enjoys it.
She calls her Korean tutor every week.
She watches Korean dramas and enjoys them very much.
Yuna cooked food from Korea.
She celebrates holidays in Korea and enjoys the fun.
She reclaims the places where her family felt safe.
She reclaims her heritage and feels proud.
She finds herself again and feels strong.
Years later, Yuna thinks about her journey and feels happy.
Yuna thinks about the girl who threw away her kimchi.
She thought about the teenager who tried hard to be white.
She thought about the young woman who learned Korean by herself.
She thinks about the person who climbed a mountain in Korea.
Yuna knows reconnecting with her heritage is not easy.
It was painful and hard to forget.
It was confusing and hard to understand.
It was lonely for a long time.
It was the most important journey of her life.
Yuna reconnects with her heritage and finds herself again.
That was something no one could take from her.
